---
title: "Building a Marketing Data Warehouse & Dashboard"
subtitle: "BigQuery, SQL, & Looker Studio"
author: "Jarrod Griffin"
institute: "College of Business Administration - Cal Poly Pomona - CCIDM"
date: "today"
date-format: "M/D/YYYY"
format:
  revealjs:
    center: false
    logo: "logos/ccidm-cpp logo.png"
    theme: [default, theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 1
    code-link: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    #chalkboard: true #cannot use with self-contained
    embed-resources: true
    multiplex: true
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.1
    minScale: 0.2
    maxScale: 2.0
    scrollable: true
    incremental: true
    navigation-mode: linear

---

## Introduction: The New Marketing Reality

::: {.callout-tip}
## Live slides available at <https://ccidm.github.io/marketing-data-warehouse-dashboard/marketing-data-warehouse-dashboard-slides.html>
:::

**The Modern Marketing Problem:** We have too much data and not enough time. If you are manually copy-pasting metrics into Excel every Friday, you are losing to automation.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
### 🛑 The "Spreadsheet" Way
- **Siloed Data:** GA4, Google Ads, and budgets live in completely different windows.
- **Fragile Tools:** Excel crashes or freezes when you hit 1,000,000 rows.
- **Manual Labor:** Reactive, static reporting that is outdated the moment you hit "Send."
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
### 🚀 The "Cloud" Way
- **Single Source of Truth:** All platforms joined together in one central hub.
- **Infinite Scale:** Cloud databases process millions of events in seconds.
- **Automated Intelligence:** Live dashboards that update themselves while you sleep.
:::
:::

::::

<br>

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
**🔥 The Career Impact:** Today, we move from **Marketing Theory** (The Art) to **Marketing Operations** (The Science). Mastering SQL and cloud architecture instantly separates you from 90% of entry-level applicants.
:::

::: {.notes}
"Welcome everyone. So, why are we here today? 

In your standard classes, you spend a lot of time on Marketing Theory—the 4 Ps, consumer behavior, segmentation. That is the 'Art' of marketing. But today, we are talking about the 'Science.'

[CLICK to reveal the Old Way]
The reality of modern marketing is that it produces massive, terrifying amounts of data. If you go into your first job and try to manage millions of clicks, impressions, and purchases in Excel, it will crash. Furthermore, you'll spend your entire Friday manually copy-pasting numbers from GA4 to Google Ads just to build a report. 

[CLICK to reveal the Cloud Way]
Today, we are moving you out of the 'Spreadsheet Way' and into the 'Cloud Way.' We are going to build a system where all your data lives in one place, scales infinitely, and updates automatically. 

[CLICK to reveal Career Impact]
Employers are desperate for marketers who don't just understand ad copy, but who know how to engineer data pipelines. By the end of this session, you'll be moving from a 'Spreadsheet Cruncher' to a 'Data Automator.' Let's get started."
:::

## Agenda & Data Roadmap

**Our Pipeline for Today:** 
`GA4 (Live Data) & CSV (Budgets)` ➡️ `BigQuery (Storage & SQL)` ➡️ `Looker Studio (Dashboard)`

- **Introduction & Orientation (5 Min)**
- **Conceptual Framework (10 Min):** The Modern Data Stack
- **Technical Implementation (15 Min):** BigQuery Orientation & Data Import
- **Querying Data (25 Min):** Core SQL Patterns & AI Assistance (Gemini)
- **Modeling for Reporting (5 Min):** Creating Views 
- **Visualization (15 Min):** Building a Looker Studio Dashboard

::: {.notes}
"Here is our roadmap for the next 75 minutes.

1.  **Conceptual Framework:** We’ll start with the big picture—why we are moving out of the GA4 interface and into the cloud.
2.  **Technical Implementation:** This is the hands-on part. We will set up a free BigQuery sandbox and upload our offline campaign budgets.
3.  **Querying Data:** We will write actual SQL code to ask questions of our data, and we'll learn how to use AI—specifically Gemini—as a coding partner.
4.  **Modeling for Reporting:** We’ll learn how to package our code into a 'View' to save processing costs.
5.  **Visualization:** Finally, we’ll connect our database to Looker Studio and build an automated dashboard."
:::

---

## What Exactly is GA4? 
### The Event-Driven Paradigm Shift

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**What is Google Analytics 4?**
GA4 is Google’s current web and app tracking platform. It logs what users do when they visit your site, where they came from, and what they buy.
:::
::: {.fragment}
**The Old Way (Universal Analytics):**
Historically, analytics tracked **"Sessions"** (a block of time a user spent on a site). Data was rigid and heavily aggregated.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**The GA4 Way: Everything is an Event!**
In GA4, the concept of a session takes a back seat. Now, **every single interaction is an Event.**
 
- Loading a page? `page_view` event.
- Scrolling down? `scroll` event.
- Buying a shirt? `purchase` event.

:::
::: {.fragment}
**Why this matters for SQL:** Because GA4 just dumps millions of these raw events into BigQuery, it creates highly complex, nested data (Arrays) that requires SQL to unpack.
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
"Before we talk about leaving GA4, we need to understand what it actually is and how it tracks data. 

If you used the old Google Analytics a few years ago, it was 'Session-Based.' You looked at a block of time. 

GA4 changed the entire marketing world because it is 'Event-Based.' Every single thing a user does—clicking a button, scrolling, viewing a page, adding to cart—is tracked as an individual event. 

This is incredibly powerful for tracking custom user journeys, but it creates a massive, complex stream of raw data. As we'll see later when we write our SQL, extracting specific details from this endless river of events is exactly why we need a cloud database."
:::

## Why Leave the GA4 Interface?
### The "Big Picture" Business Case

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**🚨 The Limits of GA4 (The UI)**

- **Data Expiration:** GA4 deletes user-level data after **14 months**. You lose year-over-year historical context.
- **The "Black Box":** The GA4 UI uses Machine Learning to model and estimate missing data. You aren't always seeing raw truth.
- **Rigid Templates:** You are limited to Google's pre-made reports. You cannot easily blend in your offline CRM or ad spend data.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**✅ The Power of the Cloud (BigQuery + Looker)**

- **Forever Ownership:** You own the data. Build 5-year trend lines without worrying about expiration.
- **Raw Truth:** BigQuery exports the un-modeled, raw event data. No "estimations."
- **Ultimate Flexibility:** If you can write the SQL, you can build any custom metric or dashboard your CMO asks for.
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
CFU (Check for Understanding): Ask the room, "If your CMO asks you to compare this Black Friday's user behavior to Black Friday from two years ago, can you do it in GA4?" (Answer: No, it's deleted. This is why we need BigQuery). Explain that in GA4, ML is used to process data, which is then shown to you. If you want to investigate anomalies further, you have to pull your data out of their templates.
:::

---

## The Cloud Landscape: Where Does BigQuery Fit?
### Demystifying "The Cloud"

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**The "Big Three" Cloud Providers**

When companies say they "use the cloud," they are just renting massive computers from one of three tech giants:

1. **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** *(Market Leader)*
2. **Microsoft Azure**
3. **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** *(What we are using today)*
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**What is BigQuery?**
BigQuery is a specific product *inside* Google Cloud. It is a fully managed **Enterprise Data Warehouse**.

- **The AWS Equivalent:** Amazon Redshift
- **The Azure Equivalent:** Azure Synapse
- **Independent Competitor:** Snowflake
:::

::: {.fragment}
*Think of Google Cloud as the giant digital office building, and BigQuery is the massive, ultra-secure filing cabinet we are renting inside of it.*
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
"Before we actually log into Google Cloud, let's establish what it is. 

In job interviews, you will hear companies say 'We are on AWS' or 'We use Google Cloud.' All this means is they are renting server space instead of buying physical computers for their basement. 

Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is Google's version of AWS. 

BigQuery is just one specific tool inside of GCP. It is a Data Warehouse. If an interviewer asks you if you know AWS Redshift or Snowflake, you can confidently say, 'I have hands-on experience with Google BigQuery, which is Google's equivalent cloud data warehouse.' 

That instantly proves to hiring managers that you understand enterprise data architecture."
:::

## Learning Outcomes:

After taking this workshop, participants should be able to:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
1.	**Create a Free Marketing Database:** Set up a Google BigQuery "Sandbox" environment to store data without needing a credit card.
2.	**Combine Multiple Data Sources:** Upload spreadsheet data (campaign budgets) into the cloud and merge it with live Google Analytics data.
3.	**Ask Questions with Code:** Use basic SQL commands (like `SELECT` and `WHERE`) to filter data and find specific answers.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
4.	**Calculate Real ROI:** "Join" your ad spend data with your performance data to calculate true metrics like Cost Per Acquisition (CPA).
5.	**Use AI as a Coding Partner:** Use Gemini inside BigQuery to write complex SQL code for you and check it for errors.
6.	**Build an Automated Dashboard:** Connect Looker Studio to your database to create a visual report that updates automatically.
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
"If you zone out, here is what you need to walk away with.

On the left, we have the **Data Engineering**: You will learn how to spin up a free cloud database, merge offline budget CSVs with live website data, and write basic SQL to ask questions of that data.

On the right, we have the **Business Intelligence**: You’ll learn how to calculate true ROI by joining datasets, use AI to write the hard code for you, and publish an automated Looker Studio dashboard so you never have to build a weekly report manually again.

If you can do these six things, you have highly technical, resume-ready skills."
:::

---

## Who Needs This Workshop?

Digital marketing roles fall into **4 categories**, and each needs this data stack differently.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
### 1. Content & Creative
*(Writers, Social, Designers)*

* **Why:** You need to see how your creative performs over time. Dashboards built in **Looker Studio** allow you to filter by specific campaigns to prove your assets drive revenue.

:::

::: {.fragment}
### 2. Technical & Performance
*(SEO, PPC, Email Specialists)*

* **Why:** You need **SQL** to join what you spent in Google Ads with the actual purchases in GA4 to calculate true Cost Per Acquisition (CPA).

:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
### 3. Data & Analytics
*(Analysts, Data Scientists)*

* **Why:** This is your core job. You need to master **BigQuery** to clean raw data, write efficient `VIEW`s, and manage the "single source of truth" for the whole company.

:::

::: {.fragment}
### 4. Strategy & Leadership
*(Managers, CMOs)*

* **Why:** You make high-stakes budget decisions. You cannot wait for a manual Excel report on Friday. You need live, automated **Dashboards** to see where the money is going today.

:::

:::

::::

::: aside
Source: [CPP MSDM Career Outlook - Typical Job Titles](https://www.cppmsdm.com/career_outlook_salary_digital_marketing#typical-job-titles)
:::

::: {.notes}
"I often hear, 'I'm a creative, I don't need to know SQL or databases.' That is incorrect. Digital marketing roles fall into four categories, and *all* of them rely on this stack.

1.  **Content & Creative:** If you design ads, you need **Looker Studio** dashboards to prove your specific creative drove revenue, not just clicks.
2.  **Technical & Performance:** If you do PPC, you need **SQL** to join your Google Ads spend with your GA4 conversion data. That's the only way to calculate true Cost Per Acquisition.
3.  **Data & Analytics:** For the analysts, **BigQuery** is your home. You are the ones building the 'views' and pipelines that the rest of the company relies on.
4.  **Strategy & Leadership:** For the future managers, you hold the budget. You can't make decisions based on last week's Excel sheet. You need the live **Automated Dashboards** we are building today."
:::

---

## Conceptual Framework
### How Cloud Databases Actually Work

**Columnar Storage & The Cost of Querying:**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**Excel (Row-Based):**

Reads data row by row, left to right. Great for finding *one specific customer's* entire profile.
:::
::: {.fragment}
**BigQuery (Columnar):**

Stores data by columns. If you only want to calculate "Total Revenue," the computer **completely ignores** the other 100 columns.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**The Cost of BigQuery:**

- **Storage:** ~$0.02 per Gigabyte.
- **Compute (Querying):** The first **1 Terabyte (TB)** of data queried every month is **FREE**. After that, it's ~$6.25 per TB.

:::

::: {.fragment}

**The Golden Rule:** `SELECT *` scans every single column in the database. In the real world, this costs your company real money. **Only SELECT the columns you need!**
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Explain Columnar storage with a visual aid: Imagine a phone book. If I ask you "Find John Smith's number," you scan row-by-row. But if I ask "What is the most common area code in the whole book?", you don't care about names or addresses. Columnar databases let the computer instantly rip out just the "Area Code" column and do the math, saving massive amounts of computing power and money.

Reassure them on costs: 1 Terabyte is a MASSIVE amount of data for free every single month. We will stay well within the free tier today.
:::

---


## Hands-On: BigQuery Orientation

### Step 1: The Sandbox Setup
1. Open an **New or Incognito Window** (to ensure a clean login).
2. Navigate to [**Google Cloud Console**](https://console.cloud.google.com).
3. Create a new Project: `ccidm-spring2026-sandbox`.
4. Pin **BigQuery** to your navigation menu.

*No credit card required for Sandbox environments!*

::: {.callout-tip}
[BigQuery Sandbox Setup Documentation](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/sandbox)
:::


::: {.notes}
Have everyone open their laptops and follow along. Tell them to open an Incognito window so they don't get mixed up with their personal Google accounts if they have multiple.
Reassure them heavily here: The "Sandbox" environment is completely free and requires no credit card. It is perfect for learning without fear of generating a massive cloud bill. Pause and make sure everyone gets to the BigQuery interface.
:::

---

## Understanding Ingestion Pathways

How does data get into our warehouse?

- **GA4 Export:** Free, daily export of raw, event-level data.
- **Manual Upload:** Manually upload data files (CSVs).
  - *Examples:* Lookup Tables, Financial Targets, Budgets
- **Google Ads Data Transfer Service (DTS):** 
  - *Why do we need this if Ads is already linked to GA4?*
  - GA4 gives session/attribution data.
  - DTS gives impressions, clicks, and *cost* at the ad-group level.


::: {.notes}
Briefly explain how enterprise data flows. 
Address a common point of confusion: "If GA4 is linked to Google Ads, why do I need a separate connection from Ads to BigQuery?" 
Answer: Because GA4 only cares about what happened on the website (sessions). To calculate ROAS and CPA, we need the actual money spent (cost), impressions, and clicks—which only comes directly from the Ads Data Transfer Service (DTS). If you are using MSDM data later in your coursework, this is exactly how it arrives.
:::

---

## Hands-On: Connecting GA4 to BigQuery

**The Native Export Pipeline**

1. Open [**Google Analytics 4**](https://analytics.google.com/analytics/web/) > Go to **Admin** (gear icon).
2. Scroll down to **Product Links** > Click **BigQuery Links**.
3. Click **Link** and choose your Google Cloud Project.
4. Select your Data Streams and Frequency:
   - **Daily:** Free, exports once a day. *(Choose this!)*
   - **Streaming:** Real-time, but costs money. 

*Note: Data takes 24 hours to appear. For today's querying, we will use a Google public dataset!*

::: {.notes}
"Now that we understand the pathways, let's actually build the bridge. 

If you have a GA4 property, go to Admin, scroll down to Product Links, and hit BigQuery Links. It is literally just a few clicks to connect them.

A critical warning for your careers: Always choose the 'Daily' export unless your company specifically needs (and pays for) real-time data. The daily export is completely free.

Because it takes 24 hours for the first batch of data to arrive, we can't query our own live data today. Instead, for the next section, we are going to use a massive public dataset provided by Google so we all have rich data to play with."
:::

---



## Hands-On: Manual Upload

Let's bring in our offline campaign budgets.

1. Create a **Dataset** called `marketing_data`.
2. Click **Add > Local File**.
3. Upload [`campaign_budgets.csv`](campaign_budgets.csv){download="campaign_budgets.csv"}.
4. Check **"Auto-detect schema"**.
5. Name the table `budget_lookup`.

::: {.notes}
Guide them through the UI. Have them download the CSV file provided for the workshop. 
Explain "Auto-detect schema"—this is BigQuery smartly figuring out if a column is text, a number, or a date without us having to code it manually.
:::

## Hands-On: Google Ads Data Transfer Service

**The Automated Pipeline:**

1. In Google Cloud Console, navigate to **BigQuery** > **Data Transfers**.
2. Click **+ Create Transfer**.
3. Choose **Google Ads** as the source type.
4. Input your **Google Ads Customer ID** and select your `marketing_data` dataset.
5. Set the schedule to **Daily** and click Save.

::: {.fragment}

::: {.callout-important}
## Where is the Google Ads Data today?
Because we are querying Google's public `ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce` dataset, we cannot link it to a live Google Ads account. Furthermore, Google **does not** provide a matching public Google Ads dataset for this GA4 data. 

To make our ROI math work today, our `campaign_budgets.csv` file is **simulating** the cost data that this Data Transfer Service would normally generate for you!
:::

:::

::: {.notes}
"Earlier, I mentioned that we use the Google Ads Data Transfer Service to get our true ad spend into BigQuery. Setting it up is incredibly easy—it’s just five clicks in the Data Transfers menu. 

However, you might be asking: 'If it's so easy, why did we just manually upload a CSV file for our budgets?'

The answer is sample data availability. We are using Google's public GA4 dataset today so we all have millions of rows to play with for free. But Google does not provide a matching public Google Ads dataset to go with it. 

So, in this workshop, the CSV we just uploaded is acting as a stand-in. In the real world, you wouldn't upload a CSV; you would set up this Data Transfer, and BigQuery would automatically create daily tables with your exact ad spend, impressions, and clicks to join with your GA4 data.

"Why do we set this to Daily instead of hourly? Two reasons. First, earlier we set our GA4 export to Daily. Since we are joining these two tables together to calculate ROI, our dashboard can only be as fast as our slowest dataset. Second, Google Ads data needs time to 'settle.' Google often refunds fraudulent clicks or processes delayed conversions hours later. Pulling the data once a night ensures we are reporting on clean, settled numbers without racking up massive cloud computing bills.""
:::

---


## Accessing our GA4 Sample Data
### Getting Google's Public E-commerce Dataset

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**Method 1: The Direct Link**

The fastest way to load the dataset is to follow this direct link straight to the Google Cloud Console:

[**🔗 Open GA4 Sample Dataset**](https://console.cloud.google.com/bigquery?ws=!1m4!1m3!3m2!1sbigquery-public-data!2sga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce)
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
**Method 2: The Explorer Pane**

If you want to find it manually inside BigQuery:

1. Go to the **Explorer** pane on the left side of your screen.
2. Click **+ Add** (Add Data).
3. Scroll to the bottom and click **Star a project by name**.
4. Input `bigquery-public-data` and press Enter.
5. Expand the newly pinned project and locate `ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce`.
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
"Now that we know how data gets into BigQuery, let's load up the sample dataset we will be querying today. 

Since our own GA4 connection takes 24 hours to generate its first export, Google provides a massive, real-world e-commerce dataset for us to practice on right now.

You have two ways to access it. You can either click the direct link in the slides, or you can use the Explorer pane to 'star' Google's public data project. I highly recommend the Explorer method (Method 2) because it teaches you how to navigate the BigQuery interface, and it pins all of Google's public datasets to your sidebar for future projects."
:::


---

## Hands-On: Your First Query 


### What is SQL?

::: {.fragment}

**SQL (Structured Query Language)** is how we talk to databases. Today we are using **GoogleSQL** (BigQuery's specific dialect). Before we type anything, let's look at the basic "grammar" of a query. If you know these three commands, you know 80% of SQL.

:::

* **`SELECT` (The Columns):** What data do I want to see? Think of this as hiding or showing columns in an Excel sheet.
* **`FROM` (The Address):** Exactly which filing cabinet and folder is this data in?
* **`WHERE` (The Rows):** How do I filter this data? Think of this as clicking the funnel icon in Excel to only show specific rows.

::: {.fragment}

```sql
SELECT 
  event_name,
  traffic_source.medium, 
  traffic_source.source,
  event_date
FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_20201203`
WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
LIMIT 10;
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Pacing: Have everyone type this manually. Do not let them copy-paste. Walk the room. 

CFU (Check for Understanding): Ask the room: "Who can tell me what would happen if we deleted the `LIMIT 5` line?" (Answer: The query would return thousands of rows, taking longer and processing more bytes, which costs more money).

Presenter trick: Intentionally make an error while typing this on screen (e.g. type `Selct` instead of `SELECT`). Let the red error box appear, and ask the class: "Can anyone read this error and tell me what I did wrong?" Emphasize that debugging is 80% of coding.
:::

---

### Finding Your Data's "Address"

Think of BigQuery's structure just like the **file system on your laptop**:

<br>

1. **Project** 💻 *(The Hard Drive - e.g., `ccidm-workshop`)*
2. **Dataset** 📁 *(The Folder - e.g., `marketing_data`)*
3. **Table** 📊 *(The Spreadsheet File - e.g., `budget_lookup`)*

<br>

::: {.fragment}

**Why does this matter?**  
When you write a SQL query, the computer doesn't magically know where your data is. You have to give it the full "address" by connecting all three levels with periods: `` `project.dataset.table` ``

:::

::: {.fragment}

```sql
-- Telling the database exactly which "file" to open:
SELECT * 
FROM `ccidm-spring2026.marketing_data.budget_lookup`
```

:::

::: {.notes}
"Students often get lost in cloud terminology. Let's make it simple. BigQuery organizes data exactly like your Mac or PC does.

The Project is your entire hard drive. The Dataset is just a folder you created on that hard drive. And the Table is the actual Excel spreadsheet sitting inside that folder.

Why do we care about this hierarchy? Because when we start writing SQL in a few minutes, you have to tell the computer exactly where to look. You do this in the FROM statement.

Look at the code on the screen. Notice how we connect the address: HardDrive dot Folder dot File. If you remember this structure, you'll never get a 'Table Not Found' error when querying, because you'll always know how to give BigQuery the correct map to your data."
:::

---

## Querying Data with SQL
### Core SQL Patterns

Now let's expand our query. We will add math and grouping.

* **SELECT:** What do I want?
* **FROM:** Where is it?
* **WHERE:** How do I filter it?
* **GROUP BY:** How do I aggregate it?

::: {.fragment}

```sql
SELECT 
  traffic_source.medium,
  SUM(ecommerce.purchase_revenue) as total_revenue
FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
GROUP BY traffic_source.medium
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

:::



::: {.fragment}

**What is the `*` doing in the FROM statement?**
BigQuery partitions (splits) GA4 data into a new table for every single day. The `*` is a **Wildcard**. It tells BigQuery: *"Look at all the days at once."*

:::

::: {.notes}
Go ahead and modify your base script to match this one and hit 'Run'. While it runs, let's break down what we just did.

If you master these four lines, you know 90% of the SQL you will ever need in your marketing career.

SELECT (Choosing Columns): Think of this like hiding or deleting columns in Excel. We don't want the user's IP address, browser, or timestamp right now. We only want two columns: the Traffic Medium, and the sum of the Revenue.

FROM (The Filing Cabinet): This tells BigQuery exactly which room and filing cabinet to open. Notice it has three parts separated by periods: the Project, the Dataset, and the Table.

WHERE (The Filter): This is exactly like clicking the filter drop-down in Excel. GA4 tracks every single click, scroll, and page view. If we didn't include WHERE event_name = 'purchase', our revenue numbers would be totally broken. We are telling the database: 'Ignore everything except the people who actually bought something.'

GROUP BY (The Pivot Table): This is the ultimate marketer's tool. In our raw data, if 100 different people bought something from an 'organic' Google search, they are listed as 100 separate rows. GROUP BY acts exactly like an Excel Pivot Table—it squishes all 100 rows into a single 'organic' row, and adds all their revenue together.
:::

---

## How SQL Actually Thinks
### Lexical vs. Logical Order

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**How we type it:**

1. `SELECT`
2. `FROM`
3. `WHERE`
4. `GROUP BY`
5. `ORDER BY`
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**How the computer reads it:**

1. `FROM` (Finds the table)
2. `WHERE` (Filters the rows)
3. `GROUP BY` (Buckets the data)
4. `SELECT` (Does the math)
5. `ORDER BY` (Sorts the final list)
:::

::::

:::{.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
GoogleSQL Syntax Refrence: [https://docs.cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/standard-sql/query-syntax](https://docs.cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/standard-sql/query-syntax)

:::
:::

::: {.notes}
"Here is a crucial concept that will save you hours of debugging. We write SQL from top to bottom, starting with SELECT. But the computer does not read it that way.

The computer starts at FROM—it has to find the filing cabinet first. 
Then it moves to WHERE to throw away rows it doesn't need. 
Then it groups the data. 
It doesn't actually look at your SELECT statement until step 4!

Why does this matter? If you rename a column 'Total Revenue' in your SELECT statement, you cannot use 'Total Revenue' in your WHERE filter. The computer will give you an error because, at the time it runs the WHERE filter, it hasn't read the SELECT statement yet and doesn't know what 'Total Revenue' means!"
:::

---

## The "Marketer's Join"
### Calculating True ROI

Let's combine our live web performance with our static budgets using a `WITH` clause to create a temporary table!

:::{.fragment}

```sql
WITH ga4_aggregate AS (
  SELECT 
    traffic_source.medium AS channel,
    COUNT(event_name) AS purchases
  FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
  WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
  GROUP BY traffic_source.medium
)

SELECT 
  b.channel,
  ga.purchases,
  b.spend,
  ROUND((b.spend / ga.purchases), 2) AS CPA
FROM `marketing_data.budget_lookup` AS b
LEFT JOIN ga4_aggregate AS ga
  ON b.channel = ga.channel
ORDER BY CPA ASC;
```

:::

::: {.notes}
> Alright everyone, this is the main event. We call this **'The Marketer's Join.'** Up until now, we’ve looked at our GA4 performance data, and we’ve looked at our offline budget CSV. Now, we are going to smash them together to calculate our true Cost Per Acquisition (CPA) on the fly.
>
> Let's break this code down into three main parts:

### 1. The `WITH` Clause (The Virtual Pivot Table)
Look at the top half of the code inside the parentheses. In the real world, GA4 data is raw—every purchase is its own row, like a giant shoebox of individual receipts.

We can't join a shoebox of receipts to our budget file. We need **totals**.

So, we use the `WITH` command. This creates what’s called a **CTE (Common Table Expression)**. You can just think of it as a temporary Pivot Table. We are telling BigQuery: *"Please count up all the purchases, bucket them by the traffic channel, and hold that data in a temporary waiting room called `ga4_aggregate`."*

### 2. The `LEFT JOIN` (Combining the Data)
Now look at the bottom half. We start our `FROM` statement with our budget CSV (`b`). 

We do a `LEFT JOIN` to bring in our temporary GA4 data (`ga`). Why a **Left** Join? Because if we spent $5,000 on Google Ads, but got **zero** purchases, we still want that $5,000 to show up on our boss's dashboard! A Left Join guarantees we don't lose our budget rows just because performance was bad.

We tell the database to match the two tables up where the channel names match (`ON b.channel = ga.channel`).

### 3. The ROI Calculation
Finally, look up at the `SELECT` statement. Because these two tables are now joined together, we can do cross-platform math!

We take our Budget Spend, divide it by our GA4 Purchases, and use the `ROUND` function to two decimal places so it looks like beautiful, readable currency.

---

**🔥 If you hit 'Run' on this, you just bypassed Excel entirely and built a fully automated ROI calculator directly in the cloud.**
:::

---

## AI-Assisted Querying (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini)

**Step 1: Get your database metadata (schema).**

AI needs context to avoid hallucinating. 

:::{.fragment}

```sql
#| code-fold: true
WITH DateRange AS (
  SELECT 
    -- 1. Use Regex to cleanly extract just the 8-digit date
    MIN(REGEXP_EXTRACT(table_name, r'^events_(\d{8})$')) AS earliest_date,
    MAX(REGEXP_EXTRACT(table_name, r'^events_(\d{8})$')) AS latest_date
  FROM 
    `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES`
  WHERE 
    -- 2. Filter out intraday tables using Regex
    REGEXP_CONTAINS(table_name, r'^events_\d{8}$')
)
SELECT 
  c.column_name, 
  c.data_type,
  d.earliest_date,
  d.latest_date
FROM 
  `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS` c
CROSS JOIN 
  DateRange d
WHERE 
  c.table_name = 'events_20210131'
-- 3. Keep the columns in their original schema order
ORDER BY 
  c.ordinal_position;
```

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Step 2: Copy the results table and give this prompt to any AI:**

> *"I am querying a BigQuery GA4 dataset. Attached is my schema. Write a query using the `events_*` wildcard table. Find the total number of 'purchase' events grouped by the user's device category. Be sure to include a date range to limit data costs."*

:::

. . .

**Always validate the logic before running!**

::: {.notes}
Introduce AI as a coding partner, not a crutch. 

Built-in AI panels (like BigQuery's native Gemini) can sometimes be buggy, unavailable, or require paid enterprise tiers. The most powerful skill you can learn is how to prompt *any* LLM (ChatGPT, Claude, standalone Gemini) to help you write SQL.

Explain that `INFORMATION_SCHEMA` is the "dictionary" of the database. It tells the computer the exact column names and data types. If you don't give an AI this dictionary, it will hallucinate column names that don't exist.

Have the room run the metadata query, click "Copy" on the results table, and paste it into ChatGPT or Claude.

Explain that GA4 stores custom data in "Arrays" (lists within lists), requiring `UNNEST`. It's incredibly hard for beginners to write, but AI is great at it *if* it has the schema.

Remind them: AI is just a predictive text engine. Always read the code it gives you before you hit run!
:::

---

## Modeling for Reporting
### The Bridge to BI: Creating a View

:::{.fragment}

**The Problem:** Looker Studio is a visualization layer, not a data transformation engine. If we force Looker to perform our complex `LEFT JOIN` on the fly, the dashboard will be incredibly slow.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**The Solution:** A view is a saved SQL query that acts as a "virtual table." We do the heavy lifting in BigQuery and save it. When Looker Studio connects, it sees a clean, Excel-like table.

:::

:::{.fragment}

```sql
#| code-fold: true
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW `marketing_data.v_campaign_performance` AS 
WITH ga4_aggregate AS (
  SELECT 
    PARSE_DATE('%Y%m%d', event_date) AS date, -- Turns "20210131" into a real Date
    traffic_source.medium AS channel,
    COUNT(event_name) AS purchases,
    SUM(ecommerce.purchase_revenue) AS revenue
  FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
  WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
  GROUP BY date, channel
)

SELECT 
  ga.date,
  b.channel,
  ga.purchases,
  b.spend,
  ga.revenue
FROM `marketing_data.budget_lookup` AS b
LEFT JOIN ga4_aggregate AS ga
  ON b.channel = ga.channel
ORDER BY ga.date DESC;
```

:::

::: {.notes}
Explain the new addition: `PARSE_DATE`. GA4 doesn't store dates as dates; it stores them as text strings (`20210131`). If we don't parse it, Looker Studio won't let us build a timeline.

**Crucial Data Engineering Concept (The Fan-Out):**
Notice we removed the `CPA` calculation from this SQL. Why? Because our budget CSV only had one row per channel with the *total* spend, but our GA4 data now has *daily* rows. 
When we joined them, BigQuery duplicated the total budget onto every single day! If we don't handle this carefully in Looker Studio, our total spend will look 30x larger than it actually is. This is why in the real world, budget files should always include a Date column so we can join on BOTH Channel and Date!
:::

---

## Visualizing in Looker Studio
### Step 1: The Connection

1. Go to [**lookerstudio.google.com**](https://lookerstudio.google.com).
2. Create a **Blank Report**.
3. Select **BigQuery** as your data connector.
4. Navigate to: `ccidm-spring2026-sandbox` > `marketing_data` > `v_campaign_performance`.

::: {.notes}
Transition from data engineering back to visual analytics. 
Have everyone log into Looker Studio. Walk the room to make sure everyone successfully connects their BigQuery View to the new Looker Studio report.
:::

---

## Looker Studio: The Date Filter "Gotcha" ⚠️

When you add a **Date Range Control** in Looker Studio, you might see "No Data" or broken charts. Why?

1. **BigQuery Needs a Date Column:** Looker Studio doesn't automatically know what column represents "Time." You must ensure your SQL View parses a real date (which we did with `PARSE_DATE`).
2. **The Filter Frame:** Looker Studio applies a filter *on top* of the data BigQuery sends it. 
   - If your BigQuery data only goes up to January 31, 2021 (like our sample dataset), setting Looker Studio to "Last 7 Days" (in 2026) will show a blank dashboard!
   - **Fix:** Always set your default Looker Studio date range to match the timeframe of your underlying dataset.

::: {.notes}
Demonstrate this live. Add a date filter, set it to "Today", watch the dashboard break (because the sample data is from 2021). Then change the date filter to Jan 2021 and watch the charts magically populate. This is a massive "Aha!" moment for students debugging dashboards.
:::

---

## Visualizing in Looker Studio
### Step 2: Building the Dashboard

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Visuals to Add:**

- **Time Series:** Conversions & Spend over time.
- **Scorecards:** Total Spend, Total Revenue, Overall ROAS.
- **Controls:** Date Range, Campaign Dropdown.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Calculated Fields:**

- You can calculate `SUM(Revenue)/MAX(Spend)` directly in Looker Studio.
- *Discussion: When should we calculate in SQL vs. Looker Studio?*
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Let them have some fun building the actual dashboard visuals. 
Bring up the discussion point: "If we can calculate ROI in SQL, why do it in Looker?" 
Answer: If a metric needs to recalculate dynamically based on dashboard filters (like an aggregate Conversion Rate for only a specific date range), it's often better to calculate it in Looker Studio using SUM() formulas. If it's a static row-level calculation, do it in SQL.
:::

---

## Summary

Today we transformed from theoretical marketers to data operators.

✅ Set up a cloud database (BigQuery) <br>
✅ Uploaded and joined disparate data sources <br>
✅ Wrote SQL with AI assistance <br>
✅ Modeled a virtual table (View) <br>
✅ Automated a dynamic Looker Studio dashboard <br>

::: {.notes}
Recap the massive amount of work they just accomplished. They built a professional-grade marketing data pipeline in under two hours. Encourage them to list these specific tools (BigQuery, SQL, Looker Studio) on their resumes and LinkedIn profiles.
:::

---

## Looking Ahead: Workshop 3

**Scalable Customer & Campaign Analysis in R**

What happens when you need to run deep customer cohort analysis or funnel drop-offs, and spreadsheets crash?

Next time, we tackle "Big-ish Data" on your local machine using **R, DuckDB, and Arrow**. 

::: {.notes}
Tease the next workshop. Dashboards are great for monitoring, but what about deep analytics? 
Next time, we move out of SQL and into R to do advanced cohort tracking and funnel analysis using local database engines.
:::

---

## Q&A and Resources

**Thank You!**

**Helpful Links:**

- [BigQuery Sandbox Setup](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/sandbox)
- [GA4 BigQuery Export Schema](https://support.google.com/analytics/answer/7029846)
- [Write SQL with Gemini](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/write-queries-with-gemini)
- [Looker Studio Connection Guide](https://support.google.com/looker-studio/answer/6370296)

*Center for Customer Insight and Digital Marketing*

::: {.notes}
Open the floor for any final questions. Tell them they can access these links in the workshop materials to reference later. Thank them for coming!
:::
